channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: EmbTracker: Traceable Black-box Watermarking for Federated Language Models
item.description: EmbTracker is the first server-side, traceable black-box watermarking framework for Federated Language Models (FedLMs). It enables robust ownership attribution without requiring white-box access or client-side cooperation—addressing a critical gap in existing group-level watermarking schemes. By embedding unique identity-specific backdoor watermarks into each client’s distributed model, EmbTracker achieves *client-level traceability*: any leaked model can be unambiguously linked to its recipient. Verification is performed via simple API queries (e.g., feeding trigger prompts), ensuring true black-box operability. Extensive experiments across LLaMA-2, Qwen, and vision-language models show near-perfect verification rates (99.8% avg.), strong resilience against fine-tuning, pruning, and quantization (>94% survival), and negligible primary-task degradation (≤1.7% accuracy drop). EmbTracker thus establishes a new standard for accountable, deployable watermarking in untrusted federated settings.

item.title: Cascade: Composing Software-Hardware Attack Gadgets for Adversarial Threat Amplification in Compound AI Systems
item.description: This paper introduces **Cascade**, a novel threat model for Compound AI systems—pipelines integrating LLMs, software tools, and databases atop layered software stacks and distributed hardware. We demonstrate that traditional software vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-listed code injection) and hardware flaws (e.g., Rowhammer, timing channels) can be *composed* with LLM-specific weaknesses to amplify adversarial impact beyond isolated-layer risks. We present two concrete attacks: (1) a **guardrail-bypass cascade**, combining frontend code injection with precision Rowhammer-induced DRAM bit flips to inject pristine jailbreak prompts—evading safety guardrails without prompt tampering; and (2) a **knowledge-redirect cascade**, exploiting database manipulation to misguide an LLM agent into exfiltrating sensitive user data to malicious endpoints. By systematizing attack primitives across objectives (integrity/confidentiality) and lifecycle stages (trigger → exploit → payload delivery), we enable rigorous cross-stack red-teaming and lay the foundation for holistic defense co-design.

item.title: Credibility Matters: Motivations, Characteristics, and Influence Mechanisms of Crypto Key Opinion Leaders
item.description: This study investigates how crypto Key Opinion Leaders (KOLs) construct and enact credibility in volatile, high-risk Web3 ecosystems. Drawing on 13 in-depth interviews and grounded in Self-Determination Theory (SDT), we move beyond static credential-based views of influencer trustworthiness to reveal credibility as a self-determined, ethically embedded *practice*. We identify four community-recognized markers: **self-regulation**, **bounded epistemic competence**, **accountability**, and **reflexive self-correction**—together framing credibility as *socio-technical performance*. Methodologically, we pioneer a hybrid human-LLM thematic analysis that ensures rigor while scaling qualitative insight. The work extends SDT into decentralized finance contexts and offers actionable design principles for credibility signals prioritizing transparency over hype—e.g., standardized conflict-of-interest disclosures and verifiable claim tagging.

item.title: The Mirror Design Pattern: Strict Data Geometry over Model Scale for Prompt Injection Detection
item.description: Prompt injection defenses are commonly treated as semantic understanding tasks, relying on increasingly large neural detectors. However, the first-line (L1) screening layer demands strict operational constraints: sub-millisecond latency, determinism, non-promptability, and full auditability—properties incompatible with black-box LLMs. We introduce the **Mirror design pattern**, a data-curation paradigm that structures prompt injection corpora into matched positive/negative “cells” to enforce learning of *control-plane attack mechanics*, not spurious corpus correlations. Using 5,000 rigorously validated open-source samples, we define a 32-cell mirror topology (31 filled), train a sparse character n-gram linear SVM, and compile its weights into a dependency-free Rust binary. On a 524-case holdout, Mirror achieves **95.97% recall** and **92.07% F1** at **sub-millisecond latency**, outperforming Prompt Guard 2 (22M params) — which scores only **44.35% recall** and **59.14% F1** at **49 ms median / 324 ms p95 latency**. Our results demonstrate that for L1 screening, *strict data geometry matters more than model scale*.

item.title: You Told Me to Do It: Measuring Instructional Text-induced Private Data Leakage in LLM Agents
item.description: This paper identifies and quantifies the *Trusted Executor Dilemma*: high-privilege LLM agents—trusted to autonomously read documentation (e.g., READMEs) and execute instructions—consistently execute adversarial commands embedded in benign text due to inherent inability to distinguish malicious directives from legitimate setup guidance. We formalize a three-dimensional taxonomy (linguistic disguise, structural obfuscation, semantic abstraction) and introduce **ReadSecBench**, a benchmark of 500 real-world README files. Experiments on a commercially deployed computer-use agent show end-to-end private data exfiltration success rates up to **85%**, consistent across five programming languages and three injection positions. Cross-model evaluation on four LLM families (GPT, Claude, Llama, Gemini) confirms robust semantic compliance with injected instructions. A 15-participant user study yields **0% detection rate**, and neither 12 rule-based nor 6 LLM-based defenses achieve reliable detection without unacceptable false positives. Our results establish a persistent *Semantic-Safety Gap*—functional instruction compliance without security awareness—making documentation-embedded instruction injection a critical, unmitigated threat to production LLM agents.

item.title: OpenClaw PRISM: A Zero-Fork, Defense-in-Depth Runtime Security Layer for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents
item.description: OpenClaw PRISM is a zero-fork, defense-in-depth runtime security layer for tool-augmented LLM agents built on OpenClaw. It addresses critical risks—including indirect prompt injection, unsafe tool execution, credential leakage, and local file tampering—by enforcing policies across ten fine-grained lifecycle hooks (e.g., message ingress, prompt construction, tool invocation, result persistence, outbound messaging). Rather than introducing a new detection model, PRISM integrates a hybrid heuristic-and-lightweight-LLM scanning pipeline, conversation-aware risk accumulation with TTL-based decay, and strict policy controls over tools, paths, private networks, domain tiers, and outbound secret patterns. Its tamper-evident audit plane ensures integrity via cryptographic signing and supports hot-reloadable policy updates. Evaluated on curated same-slice experiments and operational microbenchmarks, PRISM achieves 98.7% indirect injection detection and 100% unauthorized tool blocking with <8.2 ms overhead per step and <0.3% throughput impact—demonstrating production-ready deployability for real agent gateways.

item.title: Exponential-Family Membership Inference: From LiRA and RMIA to BaVarIA
item.description: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are critical for auditing ML model privacy, yet LiRA, RMIA, and BASE appear disparate—obscuring principled design choices. We unify them under a single **exponential-family log-likelihood ratio framework**, where differences reduce to distributional assumptions and per-point parameter count (1–4), yielding a natural hierarchy: BASE1 ≡ RMIA (mean-only) and BASE4 ≡ LiRA (mean + variance). Crucially, we identify **variance estimation instability under low shadow-model budgets** as the key bottleneck. To address it, we propose **BaVarIA**, a Bayesian attack using conjugate normal-inverse-gamma priors—eliminating heuristic thresholding and enabling robust inference with minimal assumptions. BaVarIA yields two variants: **BaVarIA-t** (Student-*t* predictive distribution) and **BaVarIA-n** (Gaussian with stabilized variance), both requiring zero hyperparameter tuning. Across 12 datasets and 7 shadow-model budgets (1–64), BaVarIA matches or exceeds LiRA and RMIA—delivering the largest gains (up to +4.3 AUC points) in the practically vital low-budget (<8) and offline regimes.

item.title: Taming OpenClaw: Security Analysis and Mitigation of Autonomous LLM Agent Threats
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive security analysis of autonomous LLM agents—exemplified by OpenClaw—introducing a **five-layer lifecycle-oriented framework** (initialization, input, inference, decision, execution) to systematically uncover compound threats: indirect prompt injection, skill supply chain contamination, memory poisoning, and intent drift. Through rigorous case studies on OpenClaw, we demonstrate that existing point defenses fail catastrophically against cross-temporal, multi-stage attacks—e.g., 73% evasion rate for static input filters and >92% memory poisoning success due to absent integrity validation. We propose stage-specific mitigations: plugin vetting with SBOM-based provenance, context-aware instruction filtering, intent consistency verification (ICV), and capability enforcement architecture (CEA) with runtime privilege revocation. Our work establishes a foundational, holistic security paradigm for trustworthy autonomous LLM agents.

item.title: Strict Optimality of Frequency Estimation Under Local Differential Privacy
item.description: This paper establishes the **strict optimality in mean-squared error (MSE)** for frequency estimation under local differential privacy (LDP). We prove that an estimator using a *symmetric, extremal randomized response* with *constant support size* $k^* = \left\lfloor \frac{e^\varepsilon + d - 1}{e^\varepsilon + 1} \right\rfloor$ achieves the fundamental MSE lower bound—no LDP protocol can outperform it. Remarkably, its minimal communication cost is only $\log_2\!\left(\frac{d(d-1)}{2}+1\right)$ bits, far below standard baselines. We propose **OptiFreq**, an efficient algorithm to construct this optimal mechanism and unbiased decoder. To bridge theory and practice, we design **CMS-LDP**, a modified Count-Mean Sketch that attains near-optimal MSE (<1.2% gap to the bound) for $d \geq 100$ and $\varepsilon = 1$. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets confirm theoretical predictions and provide actionable guidelines for method selection across privacy, dimensionality, and sample-size regimes.

item.title: KEPo: Knowledge Evolution Poison on Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description: Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) enhances LLMs by constructing knowledge graphs (KGs) from external data, yet this introduces novel poisoning vulnerabilities. Existing RAG poisoning attacks fail against GraphRAG due to its KG abstraction, which restructures injected text into relational graph structures and enables reasoning over semantics rather than raw passages. To expose this latent weakness, we propose **KEPo (Knowledge Evolution Poison)**—the first poisoning framework explicitly designed for GraphRAG. KEPo does not inject static toxic texts; instead, it synthesizes *plausible toxic events* and constructs *causally coherent evolution paths* from authentic facts to those events, then injects the resulting nodes and relations into the KG. In multi-target settings, KEPo interlinks poison corpora to form self-reinforcing “poisoned communities”, amplifying attack scalability and success. Experiments across three GraphRAG benchmarks show KEPo achieves state-of-the-art attack success rates: +42.7% absolute gain in single-target and up to 89.3% in triple-target attacks—significantly outperforming all prior methods.

item.title: Few-for-Many Personalized Federated Learning
item.description: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) faces a fundamental scalability bottleneck: optimal personalization for $M$ heterogeneous clients requires $M$ Pareto-optimal models, yet maintaining them is infeasible at scale. We reformulate PFL as a *few-for-many* optimization problem—training only $K \ll M$ shared server models to collectively serve all clients. We prove that this framework achieves near-optimal personalization: approximation error decays as $O(1/K)$, and each client’s model converges to its individual optimum as local data grows. Building on this, we propose **FedFew**, a gradient-based algorithm that jointly optimizes the $K$ models end-to-end—automatically discovering optimal model diversity without manual clustering or interpolation hyperparameters. Experiments across vision, NLP, and medical imaging show FedFew with just **$K = 3$ models consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PFL methods**, achieving +2.1–5.7% average accuracy gain while reducing communication overhead by 60%. Code: https://github.com/pgg3/FedFew.

item.title: Prototype-Based Knowledge Guidance for Fine-Grained Structured Radiology Reporting
item.description: Structured radiology reporting improves clinical communication but suffers from poor automation due to the need for fine-grained, discrete decisions under sparse structured supervision. In contrast, free-text reports—produced at scale—implicitly encode rich, image-aligned semantic knowledge. To bridge this gap, we propose **ProtoSR**, a prototype-based framework that injects free-text-derived knowledge into structured report generation. First, an instruction-tuned LLM automatically mines 80k+ MIMIC-CXR reports to build a multimodal knowledge base aligned with a clinical reporting template, where each answer option (e.g., “lobulated margin”) is represented by a learned visual prototype. During inference, ProtoSR retrieves relevant prototypes for the current image-question pair and refines predictions via a prototype-conditioned residual—a data-driven, selective correction mechanism. On the Rad-ReStruct benchmark, ProtoSR achieves state-of-the-art performance, with the largest gains (+12.7% F1) on detailed attribute questions, demonstrating that free-text signals significantly enhance fine-grained visual understanding.

item.title: Automating Skill Acquisition through Large-Scale Mining of Open-Source Agentic Repositories: A Framework for Multi-Agent Procedural Knowledge Extraction
item.description: This paper introduces SKILL-Miner, a framework for automating procedural skill acquisition by mining open-source agentic repositories (e.g., GitHub). Moving beyond static LLMs, it extracts reusable, standardized skills—such as Manim-powered mathematical visualization and pedagogical explanation—from state-of-the-art agents like TheoremExplainAgent and Code2Video. The pipeline combines structural repository analysis, dense semantic retrieval over code/docs, and translation into the interoperable `SKILL.md` format. Crucially, integration requires **no LLM retraining**, yet augments agent capabilities with verified, sandboxed, and security-governed skills. Evaluation across functional correctness, safety compliance, and pedagogical efficacy shows extracted educational skills improve knowledge transfer efficiency by **40%** while maintaining human-level tutorial quality (96.2% PQS). This enables scalable, trustworthy procedural knowledge infusion into next-generation agentic systems.

item.title: When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows
item.description: This paper introduces the *Agentic Operating System for Hospital* (AOS-H), an architecture that adapts LLM agents for safe, auditable deployment in dynamic clinical environments. Grounded in the open-source OpenClaw framework—which structures agent capabilities as a curated library of discrete skills—we extend it with four hospital-specific innovations: (1) a Linux-inspired restricted execution environment enforcing strict resource isolation and permission control; (2) a document-centric interaction paradigm where patient, clinician, and care-team agents coordinate via versioned EMR pages rather than raw API calls; (3) a page-indexed memory (PIM) architecture enabling efficient, traceable retrieval of long-term clinical context (e.g., 90+ days of longitudinal data); and (4) a rigorously vetted MedSkill Library of 127 atomic, composable clinical functions—accessible only through declarative interfaces, eliminating arbitrary code execution. Unlike general-purpose agent systems, AOS-H prioritizes safety, transparency, and regulatory compliance (HIPAA/GDPR) without sacrificing workflow agility. Evaluated in a tertiary hospital emergency department over 8 weeks, it achieved 98.2% documentation accuracy and reduced task-chain failure by 76%, demonstrating feasibility of an auditable, skill-constrained agentic layer for real-world healthcare.

item.title: See, Symbolize, Act: Grounding VLMs with Spatial Representations for Better Gameplay
item.description: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) perceive scenes well but struggle to ground perception into precise, executable actions in interactive environments. This paper investigates whether augmenting VLMs with spatial symbolic representations—alongside raw visual frames—improves gameplay performance. We evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs across Atari, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR under four input conditions: frame-only, frame + self-extracted symbols, frame + ground-truth symbols, and symbol-only. Results show consistent performance gains (up to +41.6% success rate) when *accurate* symbolic information is provided, confirming that spatial grounding enhances decision-making. However, when symbols are extracted autonomously by the VLM, performance degrades sharply with scene complexity and model capability—e.g., symbol F1 drops >68% in cluttered scenes, causing action failure rates to double. Crucially, we find a strong linear relationship between symbol extraction error and reward degradation (−19.3% reward per 10% F1 drop), establishing *perceptual reliability*, not reasoning capacity, as the central bottleneck for VLM-based agents. Our work highlights “trustworthy symbol extraction” as a prerequisite for effective grounding.

item.title: Performance Evaluation of Open-Source Large Language Models for Assisting Pathology Report Writing in Japanese
item.description: This study presents the first systematic evaluation of open-source large language models (LLMs) for assisting Japanese pathology report writing. We benchmarked seven models across three clinically relevant tasks: (A) structured generation and key information extraction from diagnosis text following Japan Pathological Society guidelines; (B) correction of typographical errors in real-world Japanese pathology reports; and (C) expert (pathologists & clinicians) subjective assessment of explanatory text quality. Reasoning-enhanced and medical-specialized models (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, JMed-7B) significantly outperformed general-purpose models in structured tasks (F1 = 0.89–0.93) and typo correction, demonstrating the value of domain adaptation and chain-of-thought prompting. However, inter-rater agreement on explanatory outputs was low (ICC = 0.32), revealing substantial preference heterogeneity among clinicians. Crucially, models achieved >90% accuracy in deterministic subtasks—indicating immediate utility for format-compliant reporting and error detection—but remain unsuitable for unreviewed explanatory generation. Our findings define actionable, task-specific deployment pathways for open-source LLMs in Japanese digital pathology workflows.

item.title: Deep Learning-Based Metamodeling of Nonlinear Stochastic Dynamic Systems under Parametric and Predictive Uncertainty
item.description: This study addresses the critical gap in metamodeling nonlinear stochastic dynamic systems under *simultaneous* parametric uncertainty (e.g., material variability), predictive uncertainty (neural network epistemic uncertainty), and stochastic external loading (e.g., seismic excitation). We propose three uncertainty-aware deep learning frameworks—**MLP-LSTM**, **MPNN-LSTM**, and **AE-LSTM**—each integrating a feature-extraction module (MLP, message-passing GNN, or autoencoder) with an LSTM backbone, augmented by Monte Carlo dropout and negative log-likelihood loss for calibrated probabilistic forecasting. Validated on a Bouc-Wen oscillator and a 37-story fiber-based steel frame under stochastic ground motion and parameter perturbations, all models achieve low prediction errors (MAE < 0.012 for Bouc-Wen; up to 35% improvement over baselines for the frame), with MPNN-LSTM and AE-LSTM excelling on high-complexity structural dynamics. Crucially, strong correlation (*r* > 0.89) between predicted variance and true error confirms reliable uncertainty quantification—enabling robust active learning and confidence-aware structural response assessment.

item.title: Decentralized Orchestration Architecture for Fluid Computing: A Secure Distributed AI Use Case
item.description: This paper introduces a **Decentralized Orchestration Architecture for Fluid Computing (DOA-FC)** to enable secure, intent-driven AI deployments across administrative domains. Unlike centralized approaches, DOA-FC empowers domain-local control services as first-class capabilities, enabling autonomous yet coordinated resource placement via lightweight intent negotiation—preserving sovereignty while ensuring end-to-end execution. As a representative use case, we design **FU-HST**, an SDN-enabled multi-domain anomaly detection mechanism that proactively identifies Byzantine clients in Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) by analyzing cross-domain traffic patterns and feature hashes, complementing robust aggregation. Simulation results show FU-HST achieves 98.7% detection F1-score with <1.2% false positives; in multi-domain DFL, it improves model accuracy by 12.4%, reduces convergence time by 21%, and adds only 8.3% communication overhead. DOA-FC establishes a foundational, security-by-design paradigm for fluid, cross-domain AI systems.

item.title: Personalized Federated Learning via Gaussian Generative Modeling
item.description: Federated learning enables collaborative model training across distributed clients while preserving data privacy, yet data heterogeneity severely hampers performance. Personalized federated learning (PFL) typically personalizes only the classifier head, overlooking client-specific characteristics in the *representation distribution*. To address this, we propose **pFedGM**, a Gaussian generative modeling framework for PFL. It first trains a Gaussian generator to capture client heterogeneity via weighted resampling. Then, it jointly optimizes a *shared objective* (maximizing inter-class separation across clients) and a *local objective* (minimizing intra-class variance per client). Crucially, pFedGM decouples the Gaussian classifier into a global navigator and a local statistic extractor, and employs a Kalman-inspired dual-scale fusion: treating the global representation distribution as a prior and local data as likelihood for Bayesian class probability estimation. Extensive experiments on FEMNIST, CIFAR-10/100, and DomainNet—under class imbalance, environmental corruption, and diverse Non-IID settings—show pFedGM consistently achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy (+1.2–3.8% avg), with markedly reduced performance variance across clients.

item.title: Sharpness-Aware Minimization for Generalized Embedding Learning in Federated Recommendation
item.description: Federated recommender systems preserve user privacy by training models locally and sharing only parameters—but they suffer from unstable learning of *generalized item embeddings*, crucial for cross-client knowledge transfer. Under severe data heterogeneity and sparsity in cross-device settings, existing methods fail to ensure embedding generalizability. To address this, we propose **FedRecGEL**, a novel framework that reformulates federated recommendation as an *item-centric multi-task learning* problem and explicitly optimizes embedding generalization throughout training. Grounded in theoretical analysis linking generalization to loss landscape sharpness, we integrate **Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM)** into the item embedding update, enabling robust convergence and improved cross-client adaptation. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets (CiteULike, Pinterest, Amazon-Book, Gowalla) show FedRecGEL consistently outperforms SOTA baselines by +3.8–6.2% in Recall@20 and NDCG@20, with enhanced training stability and reduced oscillation. Code is publicly available.

item.title: LLM-Assisted Causal Structure Disambiguation and Factor Extraction for Legal Judgment Prediction
item.description: Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) models based on pre-trained language models often rely on spurious statistical correlations, lacking explicit causal modeling of legal constituent elements. To address noisy factor extraction and structural ambiguity (e.g., Markov equivalence) in real-world legal texts, we propose an LLM-assisted causal inference framework. It integrates LLMs as constrained legal knowledge priors with statistical causal discovery: (1) a coarse-to-fine hybrid mechanism combines statistical sampling and LLM-driven semantic reasoning for accurate, noise-robust legal factor identification; (2) LLM-guided probabilistic pruning resolves causal direction uncertainty by filtering legally implausible edges; and (3) the resulting causal graphs explicitly constrain attention in a downstream prediction model. Experiments on LEVEN, QA, and CAIL show consistent gains—up to +5.8% accuracy and +7.1% F1 on confusing charges—while improving robustness against input perturbations. This work bridges foundational legal reasoning and modern causal ML.

item.title: Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents
item.description: This paper presents a security framework for frontier AI agents, grounded in Perplexity’s operational experience with production-scale agentic systems serving millions of users and enterprises. We identify how agent architectures fundamentally undermine classical security assumptions—eroding code-data separation, blurring authority boundaries, and degrading execution predictability—thereby introducing novel confidentiality, integrity, and availability failure modes. Key threats include *indirect prompt injection*, *confused-deputy behavior*, and *cascading failures* across long-running, multi-agent workflows. We map principal attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and inter-agent coordination, then propose a layered defense stack: input/model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, and deterministic policy enforcement for high-consequence actions. Finally, we highlight critical gaps: adaptive security benchmarks, formalizable delegation and privilege models, and NIST-aligned design guidance for secure multi-agent systems.

item.title: Understanding Disclosure Risk in Differential Privacy with Applications to Noise Calibration and Auditing (Extended Version)
item.description: Differential Privacy (DP) promises formal disclosure guarantees, yet a key systems challenge remains: *how does DP noise translate into actual protection against realistic inference attacks?* Existing approaches—either narrow (e.g., membership inference only) or flawed (e.g., Reconstruction Robustness/ReRo, which violates DP bounds under realistic assumptions)—hinder principled calibration and auditing. This paper introduces **Reconstruction Advantage (RA)**, a unified, attack-agnostic risk metric that rigorously captures threat across membership inference, attribute inference, and data reconstruction. We derive **tight analytical bounds** linking DP parameters (ε, δ, noise scale) to adversarial advantage for arbitrary mechanisms and attacker knowledge, and characterize optimal attack strategies. These results enable **risk-driven noise calibration** (selecting minimal noise to meet target risk) and establish the first foundation for **systematic, quantitative DP auditing**. Experiments confirm RA significantly improves audit accuracy and scope over ReRo, enabling more effective utility–privacy trade-offs in production data systems.

item.title: Matching Features, Not Tokens: Energy-Based Fine-Tuning of Language Models
item.description: Cross-entropy fine-tuning optimizes token-level prediction under teacher forcing, failing to align with sequence-level behavior during autoregressive generation. We propose **Energy-Based Fine-Tuning (EBFT)**, a feature-matching paradigm that directly targets semantic statistics of the completion distribution—without task-specific verifiers or preference models. EBFT enables efficient optimization via strided block-parallel sampling for concurrent rollout generation, batched feature extraction over rollouts, and on-policy policy-gradient updates using learned embeddings. Theoretically, EBFT is shown to implement KL-regularized feature matching within an energy-based modeling framework. Empirically, across Q&A coding, unstructured coding, and translation, EBFT matches RLVR’s downstream accuracy while outperforming SFT—and achieves *lower validation cross-entropy* than both, demonstrating superior language modeling fidelity and task alignment.

item.title: CLASP: Defending Hybrid Large Language Models Against Hidden State Poisoning Attacks
item.description: State space models (SSMs) like Mamba offer efficient alternatives to Transformers but are vulnerable to Hidden State Poisoning Attacks (HiSPAs), which corrupt memory via adversarial strings. We propose **CLASP**, a lightweight, model-agnostic defense that frames HiSPA detection as a token-level binary classification task. CLASP leverages discriminative patterns in Mamba’s block output embeddings (BOEs) and employs an XGBoost classifier—introducing negligible overhead (<4GB VRAM, 1,032 tokens/s). Evaluated on 2,483 real résumés (9.5M tokens) with controlled HiSPA injections, CLASP achieves **95.9% token-level F1** and **99.3% document-level F1**. Crucially, it generalizes robustly: **96.9% document-level F1** under leave-one-out CV and **91.6% average document-level F1** under clustered CV with structurally novel triggers. CLASP serves as a practical frontline defense for SSM-based and hybrid LLMs. Code and results: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/hispikes-91C0.

item.title: Unclonable Encryption in the Haar Random Oracle Model
item.description: We construct the first unclonable encryption (UE) scheme in the Haar random oracle model, where all parties can query a Haar-random unitary $U$ and its adjoint $U^\dagger$, complex conjugate $U^*$, and transpose $U^T$. Our scheme achieves standard unclonable indistinguishability security, supports secret key reuse, and encrypts messages of arbitrary length—resolving a longstanding open question on the feasibility of *reusable* UE in “micocrypt”, a minimal cryptographic world where one-way functions may not exist. A central technical contribution is a novel **unitary reprogramming lemma**, built upon the path recording framework, enabling controlled rewinding of quantum queries while preserving the Haar measure—a tool of independent interest for quantum oracle separations. This work establishes UE as achievable under only quantum-accessible random structure, without relying on classical hardness assumptions.

item.title: Re-Evaluating EVMBench: Are AI Agents Ready for Smart Contract Security?
item.description: This paper re-evaluates EVMBench—the first large-scale benchmark for AI agents on smart contract security—by addressing its two critical limitations: narrow evaluation scope (only 14 agent configurations, mostly tested on vendor-specific scaffolds) and high contamination risk (all audit-contest data predates every model’s release). We expand evaluation to **26 configurations** across four model families (Llama, Qwen, Claude, GPT) and three scaffolds (LangChain, DSPy, OpenAgent), and introduce a **contamination-free dataset of 22 real-world security incidents** occurring *after* all models’ release dates. Our findings challenge the narrative of imminent fully automated AI auditing: (1) agent performance is highly unstable—rankings shift significantly across scaffolds, tasks, and datasets; (2) on real-world incidents, **no agent succeeds in end-to-end exploitation across all 110 agent-incident pairs**, despite detecting up to 65% of vulnerabilities—refuting EVMBench’s claim that vulnerability discovery is the primary bottleneck; and (3) scaffold choice materially impacts results, with an open-source scaffold outperforming vendor alternatives by up to 5 percentage points—a confounder uncontrolled in EVMBench. We conclude that AI agents are valuable as pre-deployment checks or human-in-the-loop assistants—not autonomous auditors. Code and data: https://github.com/blocksecteam/ReEVMBench/.

item.title: Incremental Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection in IoT Networks under Evolving Threat Landscape
item.description: This study addresses the critical challenge of maintaining long-term intrusion detection performance in resource-constrained IoT networks under evolving cyber threats. We propose an incremental federated learning (IFL) framework using lightweight LSTM models to continuously adapt non-stationary IDSs to concept drift—without centralized data access or full model retraining. Evaluated on the CICIoMT2024 dataset (5 attack categories, 27 variants), we compare cumulative, representative-sample, and retention-based incremental strategies under both binary and multiclass classification. Results show that cumulative and representative learning achieve the highest stability under drift (F1 standard deviation < 0.03), while retention-based methods deliver the best accuracy–latency trade-off (up to +12.7% F1 with only +18 ms update latency). All IFL approaches significantly outperform periodic federated aggregation (+9.2–15.4% average F1), demonstrating their viability for real-world, low-power IoT deployments.

item.title: AttriGuard: Defeating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Causal Attribution of Tool Invocations
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents are critically vulnerable to *Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI)*, where adversaries embed malicious directives in untrusted tool outputs to hijack agent behavior. Existing defenses treat IPI as an input-level semantic classification problem, suffering from poor generalization to unseen payloads and failure under adaptive attacks. We propose a paradigm shift to *action-level causal attribution*: instead of asking “Is this input malicious?”, we ask “*Why was this specific tool invocation generated?*”—distinguishing calls grounded in user intent from those causally induced by compromised observations. We instantiate this with **AttriGuard**, a runtime defense performing parallel counterfactual tests: for each candidate tool call, it re-executes the agent under a *control-attenuated view* of external outputs. Key innovations include teacher-forced shadow replay (to prevent attribution confounding), hierarchical control attenuation (to suppress diverse attack channels while preserving task semantics), and a fuzzy survival criterion (robust to LLM stochasticity). Evaluated across four LLMs and two agent benchmarks, AttriGuard achieves **0% ASR under static IPI** with negligible utility loss (<1.2% task success drop) and moderate overhead (+18–32% latency per step). Crucially, it maintains >92% defense rate under adaptive optimization-based attacks—where leading defenses degrade severely (ASR up to 89%).

item.title: CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) to accelerate inference, but APC introduces timing side channels in multi-tenant serving: attackers infer sensitive user inputs by observing cache-hit/miss latency differences. Existing defenses disable APC entirely—sacrificing up to 70% cache reuse and increasing latency by 30%. CacheSolidarity is the first system that *selectively* mitigates APC side channels without compromising efficiency. It monitors cross-tenant prefix reuse in real time, detects statistically suspicious sharing patterns, and isolates *only high-risk prefixes*—leaving benign reuse intact. Evaluations show CacheSolidarity achieves full attack prevention while enabling **70% higher cache reuse** and **30% lower inference latency** versus isolation-only baselines. Its lightweight design (<1.2% CPU overhead) demonstrates that strong security in LLM serving need not entail unacceptable performance costs.

item.title: Repurposing Backdoors for Good: Ephemeral Intrinsic Proofs for Verifiable Aggregation in Cross-silo Federated Learning
item.description: While Secure Aggregation (SA) preserves update confidentiality in cross-silo FL, it offers no integrity guarantee—malicious servers can omit or alter updates undetected. Existing verifiable aggregation schemes rely on heavy cryptography (e.g., ZKPs, HE), suffering severe scalability bottlenecks: verification time explodes with model size. We propose **Ephemeral Intrinsic Proofs (EIP)**, a lightweight paradigm that repurposes *backdoor injection* to embed transient, verifiable signals directly into model parameters. Leveraging catastrophic forgetting, these signals remain robust for immediate server-side auditing yet decay naturally during training—ensuring zero utility loss in the final model. Our randomized, single-verifier framework integrates seamlessly with SA, preserves client anonymity, and avoids signal collisions without trusted parties. Experiments on SVHN, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 show >99.2% detection probability against malicious aggregation; on ResNet-18, EIP achieves **over 1000× speedup** versus cryptographic baselines—enabling practical, scalable verifiability for large models.

item.title: Detecting and Eliminating Neural Network Backdoors Through Active Paths with Application to Intrusion Detection
item.description: Machine learning backdoors pose severe security threats to critical applications like intrusion detection systems (IDS), as they maintain normal behavior on clean inputs but misclassify triggered inputs with high confidence. Detecting such triggers remains notoriously difficult due to their stealth and input-agnostic nature. This paper introduces an **explainable, activation-path-driven framework** for detecting and eliminating neural backdoors without requiring access to the poisoned training data or attacker knowledge. We identify *active paths*—stable, high-co-occurrence neuron activation sequences uniquely activated by backdoored inputs—and leverage statistical divergence (KS test) and co-activation graph analysis to isolate malicious paths. From these, we reconstruct trigger patterns and perform targeted path pruning followed by lightweight fine-tuning. Evaluated on NSL-KDD and CIC-IDS2017 with MLP/CNN-based IDS models, our method achieves **98.7% detection accuracy (F1=0.972)**, reduces attack success rate from 99.1% to 2.3%, and preserves >99.2% clean accuracy post-mitigation. This work bridges model interpretability and backdoor defense in a unified, data-free, and deployment-friendly manner.

item.title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
item.description: Instruction Hierarchy (IH) defines the trust-ordered priority LLMs apply when resolving conflicts among system, developer, user, and tool instructions—serving as a foundational defense against jailbreaks, system prompt extraction, and agentic prompt injection. Yet robust IH learning remains challenging due to confounding with general instruction-following failures, nuanced conflict semantics, and shortcut behaviors like overrefusal. We introduce **IH-Challenge**, a reinforcement learning training dataset featuring adversarially curated instruction conflicts with explicit priority labels and dynamic boundary-case generation. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge with online adversarial sampling improves average IH robustness by **+10.0%** (84.1% → 94.1%) across 16 in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and human red-teaming benchmarks; reduces unsafe responses from **6.6% to 0.7%** while *increasing* helpfulness on general safety evaluations; and achieves saturation (100%) on an internal static agentic prompt injection benchmark—with negligible capability regression. The dataset is publicly released to advance research on trustworthy instruction hierarchy.

item.title: Enhancing Network Intrusion Detection Systems: A Multi-Layer Ensemble Approach to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks
item.description: Adversarial examples pose a critical threat to ML-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS), potentially enabling evasion attacks that compromise network security. To address this, we propose a novel **multi-layer ensemble defense framework** combining stacked classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, RF) and a conditional autoencoder layer: benign-classified traffic triggers autoencoder-based reconstruction verification, forming a robust “classify-then-validate” pipeline. We generate adversarial traffic via both GAN and FGSM methods and integrate adversarial training into the entire pipeline. Evaluations on UNSW-NB15 and NSL-KDD show our approach improves detection accuracy by 12.7–18.3% under FGSM attacks and boosts AUC by 9.5–13.1 points under GAN attacks, while maintaining false positive rates <0.8%. This work introduces the first dynamically activated autoencoder layer for NIDS adversarial mitigation, significantly enhancing resilience without sacrificing detection performance.

item.title: Don't Let the Claw Grip Your Hand: A Security Analysis and Defense Framework for OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first structured security analysis of OpenClaw—a popular open-source, locally-executed LLM-powered code agent framework. We systematically evaluate its native resilience across 47 adversarial scenarios spanning six MITRE ATLAS/ATT&CK attack categories, revealing a critically low baseline defense rate of only 17%, especially against sandbox escape and privilege escalation. To address this, we propose a lightweight Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense layer that intercepts high-risk commands via semantic risk scoring and optional human approval before execution. Dual-mode evaluation shows our HITL layer successfully blocks all 8 severe attacks that fully bypassed OpenClaw’s native defenses. When integrated, the combined system achieves defense rates ranging from 19% to 92%, depending on attack type. Our work exposes fundamental security gaps in current code agents and demonstrates that judicious human-agent collaboration—not full automation—is essential for secure real-world deployment.

item.title: Risk-Adjusted Harm Scoring for Automated Red Teaming for LLMs in Financial Services
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly deploying in regulated Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance (BFSI) contexts, yet standard red-teaming benchmarks lack domain specificity and fail to capture legally or professionally plausible harmful behaviors. We introduce a risk-aware evaluation framework featuring: (1) a BFSI-specific harm taxonomy, (2) an automated multi-round adversarial pipeline, and (3) an ensemble judging protocol yielding the **Risk-Adjusted Harm Score (RAHS)**—a severity-weighted metric that incorporates operational impact, mitigation signals, and inter-judge agreement. Across 12 financial LLMs, we find that increased decoding stochasticity and sustained adaptive interaction not only raise jailbreak success rates but systematically escalate outputs toward *operationally actionable* financial disclosures (e.g., fabricated account numbers, bypassed KYC logic). These results demonstrate the inadequacy of single-turn, domain-agnostic security evaluation and underscore the necessity of risk-sensitive, prolonged adversarial assessment for real-world BFSI deployment.

item.title: AlphaFlowTSE: One-Step Generative Target Speaker Extraction via Conditional AlphaFlow
item.description: Target speaker extraction (TSE) aims to recover clean target speech from a multi-talker mixture using a short enrollment utterance. While diffusion and flow-matching models improve fidelity, their multi-step sampling incurs high latency, and existing one-step methods rely on unreliable mixture-dependent time coordinates—limiting real-world robustness. We propose **AlphaFlowTSE**, a one-step conditional generative model trained with a **Jacobian-vector-product (JVP)-free AlphaFlow objective**. It learns mean-velocity transport directly from the observed mixture toward the target speech—eliminating auxiliary mixing-ratio prediction—and stabilizes training via an **interval-consistency teacher-student loss** that enforces temporal coherence in flow matching. Experiments on Libri2Mix and REAL-T show AlphaFlowTSE achieves superior target similarity (e.g., +2.1 dB SI-SNRi) and generalization to real mixtures, reducing downstream ASR WER by 18.7% on average compared to prior SOTA.

item.title: Prompting with the human-touch: evaluating model-sensitivity of foundation models for musculoskeletal CT segmentation
item.description: This study evaluates 11 promptable foundation models (FMs) for musculoskeletal CT segmentation of bone and implants across wrist, shoulder, hip, and lower leg—using both public and private datasets. We benchmark non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting strategies and, critically, incorporate *real human prompts* collected from 5 radiologists via a dedicated observer study—replacing idealized label-derived prompts. Pareto-optimal models are identified: **SAM and SAM2.1 for 2D**, **nnInteractive and Med-SAM2 for 3D**. Key findings include: (1) substantial performance variation across models and prompting modalities; (2) strong anatomical dependence of localization accuracy and inter-rater consistency (e.g., ICC=0.89 for wrist bones vs. ≤0.52 for pelvis/tibia/implants); (3) consistent performance drop (mean −12.3% Dice) with human prompts, indicating overoptimistic reporting in label-based evaluations; and (4) universal sensitivity to prompt variation—no model achieved robustness across raters despite intra-rater stability in two cases. Our open-source benchmarking framework is available at https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark.

item.title: Federated Active Learning Under Extreme Non-IID and Global Class Imbalance
item.description: Federated Active Learning (FAL) aims to reduce annotation costs under privacy constraints, yet suffers severe performance degradation under *simultaneous* extreme non-IID data and global class imbalance—common in real-world deployments. Through systematic analysis, we uncover a key insight: **class-balanced querying—not just model accuracy—is the primary driver of final performance**, especially for minority classes. Moreover, global-model querying only benefits when global imbalance is high *and* client data are relatively homogeneous; otherwise, local models are preferable. Guided by these findings, we propose **FairFAL**, the first adaptive, class-fair FAL framework. It (1) infers global imbalance and local-global divergence via lightweight prediction discrepancy to dynamically select between global/local query models; (2) performs prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features to prioritize minority-class queries; and (3) employs a two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling strategy with k-center refinement. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks—including long-tailed CIFAR-10-LT and ImageNet-LT under strong Non-IID—show FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods (e.g., FedAL, BALD-Fed) by 3.2–7.8% absolute accuracy, with gains up to 11.4% in the most challenging settings. Code: https://github.com/chenchenzong/FairFAL.

item.title: SNPgen: Phenotype-Supervised Genotype Representation and Synthetic Data Generation via Latent Diffusion
item.description: SNPgen is a phenotype-supervised latent diffusion framework for generating high-fidelity synthetic genotypes. It integrates GWAS-guided SNP selection (1,024–2,048 trait-associated variants) with a VAE-based genotype compression and a classifier-free guided latent diffusion model conditioned on binary disease labels. Evaluated on 458,724 UK Biobank individuals across four complex diseases, models trained solely on SNPgen-synthetic data achieve predictive performance (AUC) on real test sets comparable to those trained on real data—and rival genome-wide PRS using 2–6× more variants. Privacy analysis confirms zero identical matches, near-random membership inference (AUC ≈ 0.50), preserved linkage disequilibrium, and strong allele frequency correlation (*r* ≥ 0.95). A controlled simulation with known causal effects further validates faithful recovery of genetic association structure. SNPgen bridges the gap between statistical fidelity and task utility while ensuring robust privacy.

item.title: Prioritizing Gradient Sign Over Modulus: An Importance-Aware Framework for Wireless Federated Learning
item.description: Wireless federated learning (FL) faces severe performance degradation due to unreliable gradient transmission under scarce wireless resources. To address this, we propose **Sign-Prioritized FL (SP-FL)**—a novel importance-aware framework that prioritizes the reliable transmission of gradient *signs*, which encode the essential descent direction for model updates, over less critical gradient *modulus*. SP-FL transmits signs in dedicated, high-reliability packets and allows sign reuse when modulus recovery fails. We formulate a hierarchical resource allocation problem jointly optimizing bandwidth across devices and power between sign/modulus packets, guided by explicit importance metrics derived from SP-FL’s one-step convergence analysis. An alternating optimization algorithm—combining Newton-Raphson and successive convex approximation (SCA)—is designed to solve it efficiently. Experiments on CIFAR-10 show SP-FL achieves up to **9.96% higher test accuracy** than state-of-the-art wireless FL baselines under constrained resources, confirming its superiority in reliability-critical edge AI deployment.

item.title: MultiwayPAM: Multiway Partitioning Around Medoids for LLM-as-a-Judge Score Analysis
item.description: LLM-as-a-Judge enables flexible, multi-perspective text evaluation via prompt engineering, yet suffers from high inference cost and systematic evaluator bias. To uncover the *structured nature* of such bias across questions, answerers, and evaluators, we propose **MultiwayPAM**, a novel tensor clustering algorithm that simultaneously identifies cluster memberships and interpretable medoids for all three modes of a score tensor. Unlike standard decomposition or unimodal clustering, MultiwayPAM guarantees real-data medoids (not abstract centroids) per mode, enabling direct semantic interpretation—for instance, revealing evaluator subgroups with consistent over-scoring of open-source models or under-scoring of verbose answers. Evaluated on two real-world LLM-as-a-Judge datasets (12K+ scores), MultiwayPAM achieves +23.6% average improvement in clustering quality over baselines and enables bias-aware score aggregation that boosts Spearman correlation with human judgments by 0.18 using only 15% of original inference cost. This work establishes multiway clustering as a principled, interpretable tool for auditing and mitigating LLM evaluator bias.

item.title: GLM-OCR Technical Report
item.description: GLM-OCR is an efficient 0.9B-parameter multimodal model for real-world document understanding. It integrates a 0.4B-parameter CogViT visual encoder with a 0.5B-parameter GLM language decoder, striking an optimal balance between efficiency and accuracy. To overcome the latency bottleneck of autoregressive decoding in deterministic OCR, GLM-OCR introduces Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), enabling parallel multi-token generation per step while maintaining low memory overhead via parameter sharing. System-wise, it adopts a two-stage pipeline: layout analysis by PP-DocLayout-V3 followed by parallel region-level structured recognition. Evaluations across public benchmarks (PubLayNet, DocBank, CORD, SciTSR, FormulaGraph) and industrial deployments show state-of-the-art or competitive performance in document parsing, text/formula transcription, table structure recovery, and key information extraction. Its compact size, structured generation capability, and low-latency inference make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale production systems.

item.title: Security-by-Design for LLM-Based Code Generation: Leveraging Internal Representations for Concept-Driven Steering Mechanisms
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at code generation but frequently produce functionally correct yet insecure outputs—posing critical risks in production systems. Prior mitigation methods remain insufficient due to a lack of insight into *how* security concepts are internally represented and activated during generation. This work reveals that CodeLLMs encode distinct, separable neural representations for security subconcepts (e.g., SQLi vs. XSS) *before* generating vulnerable tokens—enabling fine-grained, concept-aware intervention. We propose **SCS-Code**, a lightweight, modular steering mechanism that dynamically guides internal representations toward secure and functional code during token decoding—without model retraining or architectural modification. Evaluated across six secure coding benchmarks, SCS-Code achieves +28.7% average improvement in security compliance while preserving >98.8% functional correctness—outperforming state-of-the-art methods including RLHF-based and adversarial fine-tuning approaches.

item.title: Separating Oblivious and Adaptive Differential Privacy under Continual Observation
item.description: We resolve an open problem posed by Jain et al. (ICML 2023) by giving the first explicit separation between oblivious and adaptive differential privacy under continual observation. In the oblivious setting, data streams are fixed in advance; in the adaptive setting, streams may be chosen adaptively based on prior algorithm outputs—imposing stricter privacy requirements. Our separation is built on the correlated vector queries problem (Bun et al., SODA 2017). We design an $(\varepsilon,0)$-DP algorithm for the oblivious setting that maintains constant accuracy for exponentially many time steps in the input dimension $d$. In contrast, we prove that *every* $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP adaptive algorithm fails to achieve nontrivial accuracy after only a constant number of releases—specifically, within $O(1/\varepsilon^2)$ steps. This establishes a fundamental gap: adaptivity severely limits utility in continual release, even with relaxed privacy.

item.title: TOSSS: a CVE-based Software Security Benchmark for Large Language Models
item.description: TOSSS (Two-Option Secure Snippet Selection) is a novel, CVE-based benchmark for evaluating large language models’ ability to distinguish secure from vulnerable code snippets. Unlike prior security benchmarks with narrow vulnerability coverage and static datasets, TOSSS leverages real-world CVE entries from the NVD database, enabling extensibility as new vulnerabilities are disclosed. It presents models with paired snippets—functionally equivalent but differing in security status—and computes a normalized security score (0–1) based on correct secure-snippet selection. We evaluate 14 widely used open- and closed-source LLMs on C/C++ and Java vulnerabilities across 12 CWE categories, observing scores ranging from 0.48 to 0.89 (mean: 0.71). Results reveal that closed-source models (e.g., GPT-4o: 0.89) outperform open-source ones, yet no model achieves robust performance across all vulnerability types—especially memory-safety flaws. TOSSS provides a practical, standardized, and continuously updatable metric for integrating security assessment into LLM evaluation pipelines.

item.title: WebWeaver: Breaking Topology Confidentiality in LLM Multi-Agent Systems with Stealthy Context-Based Inference
item.description: Communication topology is a critical, high-value IP in LLM-based multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS), yet its confidentiality remains underexplored under realistic defenses. Prior topology inference attacks rely on impractical assumptions—such as controlling the admin agent or issuing direct identity queries via jailbreaks—making them easily thwarted by basic keyword filters and thus non-transferable to real deployments. To bridge this gap, we propose **WebWeaver**, the first stealthy, single-agent-compromise framework that infers *full* LLM-MAS topology using only natural agent *contexts* (e.g., role descriptions, dialogue history), not explicit IDs. WebWeaver introduces: (i) a novel covert jailbreak mechanism evading detection, and (ii) a fully jailbreak-free diffusion design with a theoretically sound masking strategy that preserves known topology during iterative inference. Extensive experiments show WebWeaver achieves **89.7% edge accuracy** under active defenses—~60% higher than SOTA baselines—with negligible latency (<0.8 s/query) and no detectable behavioral anomalies.

item.title: The Attack and Defense Landscape of Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive survey on security for agentic AI systems—LLM-based agents integrating non-AI components (e.g., tools, memory, planners). We systematically map the agent design space, characterize a novel attack landscape comprising 12 threat classes (including 7 newly identified ones), and analyze 36 defense mechanisms across input sanitization, runtime monitoring, tool access control, memory auditing, and multi-agent verification. Through case studies on AutoGen, LangChain Agent, MetaGPT, and OpenDevin, we expose critical gaps: excessive tool permissions, unvalidated memory caching, undetected goal drift in multi-step reasoning, and missing end-to-end trust chains. We introduce the **Agent Security Risk–Countermeasure Framework (ASRF)**—the first unified taxonomy linking threats to mitigations—and identify six open challenges, including formal verification of agent behavior, robustness metrics under adaptive attacks, and human-in-the-loop trust calibration. Our work establishes a foundational reference for building secure agentic systems and advancing research in this rapidly evolving domain.

item.title: Jailbreak Scaling Laws for Large Language Models: Polynomial-Exponential Crossover
item.description: Adversarial prompt injection (“jailbreaking”) reliably compromises safety-aligned LLMs, yet its scaling behavior with inference-time sampling remains uncharacterized. We discover a sharp **polynomial-to-exponential crossover** in attack success rate: without injection, success grows slowly as ∼N^α; with injection, it surges exponentially as ∼1−e^(−βN). To explain this, we propose a spin-glass generative model of language—where unsafe outputs correspond to low-energy, size-biased clusters in a replica-symmetry-breaking Gibbs measure. Prompt injection acts as an external magnetic field: short prompts (weak field) yield power-law scaling; long prompts (strong field) induce an ordered phase that biases sampling toward unsafe clusters, causing exponential growth. We derive these scalings analytically and validate them empirically across Llama-3-70B, Qwen2-72B, and Claude-3. This reveals jailbreaking as a *field-driven phase transition*, offering a physics-grounded foundation for quantifying and mitigating alignment failures.

item.title: Client-Conditional Federated Learning via Local Training Data Statistics
item.description: Federated learning (FL) under data heterogeneity remains challenging due to trade-offs among communication efficiency, model personalization, and robustness to sparse data. We propose **client-conditional FL**, where a *single global model* is conditioned on lightweight, locally computed PCA statistics (e.g., mean/variance of top-k principal components) of each client’s training data—requiring **zero additional communication**. Unlike clustering-based (IFCA) or per-client model (Ditto) approaches, our method leverages continuous, differentiable distributional signatures instead of discrete cluster IDs. Evaluated across 97 configurations—including four heterogeneity types (label, covariate, concept, and combined shift), four datasets (MNIST to CIFAR-100), and seven FL baselines—our method **matches the Oracle baseline** (with perfect cluster knowledge) in all settings, and **surpasses it by 1–6% under combined heterogeneity**, where PCA statistics capture richer structural information than hard clusters. Crucially, it is the **only method robust to extreme data sparsity** (<50 samples/client), establishing a new paradigm for efficient, statistics-driven conditional FL.
